---
title: "Chapter 3: goldbug (CA Mother Lode placer + lode Au)"
subtitle: "A live web map: ML prospectivity on federal land in the Mother Lode, filtered to acres actually open to an individual prospector"
---

[← back to portfolio map](index.qmd)

::: {.d-none .d-lg-block}
<iframe src="https://www.johnsondevco.com/goldbug/"
        style="width:100%;height:75vh;border:1px solid #ccc;border-radius:6px"
        loading="lazy"></iframe>
:::

::: {.d-lg-none .my-3}
[Open the live tool →](https://www.johnsondevco.com/goldbug/){.btn .btn-primary .btn-lg target="_blank"}

The map works best on a wider screen. Tap the button to open it in
a new tab.
:::

## What this is

A live web tool that ranks open federal parcels in California's
Mother Lode by gold prospectivity. The score combines a random
forest trained on the same kind of public USGS data the regional
pipelines in [Chapter 2](regional.qmd) use (geology, geophysics,
geochemistry, occurrence records) with several heuristic components
for distance to known producers, fault proximity, water access, and
road access.

The piece that took the most work isn't the modeling. It's the
question of which acres are actually open for an individual
prospector to file on. BLM publishes the data needed to answer
that, but it lives across roughly a dozen different endpoints with
inconsistent schemas, and several of the obvious filters are
subtly wrong.

## What got built

**Pipeline.** A Python package (`goldbug`) that fetches all the
public data for a configured bounding box, identifies parcels that
are federally administered and open to mineral entry, scores each
parcel using the ML model and heuristics, and renders the result
as an interactive map plus a GeoJSON export. The same package
also scores a single user-drawn polygon via a Cloud Run API, so a
visitor can sketch their own area on the live map and get a score
back.

**Open-land calculation.** Starting from the federal surface
estate (the BLM California Surface Management Agency layer), the
pipeline subtracts:

- Wilderness, Wilderness Study Areas, Areas of Critical
  Environmental Concern, and National Monuments / NCAs (the BLM
  National Conservation Lands hierarchy).
- NHD lakes and reservoirs over 50 acres.
- Bureau of Reclamation lands. The BLM SMA layer doesn't include
  them; the tool pulls them separately from PAD-US.
- Active BLM mining claims, filtered by the MLRS data-quality
  score so claims that BLM only geocoded to an entire 640-acre
  section don't over-erase the actual open ground.
- BLM Acquired Lands (these are not Public Domain and not subject
  to the 1872 Mining Law; closed to staking under federal law).
- Land-tenure segregations and withdrawals where the underlying
  case actually closes locatable mineral entry. Some of these
  (Power Site Reservations in particular) close the surface
  estate but explicitly preserve metalliferous mining, so the
  tool renders them with a warning but does not subtract them.

State-managed lands open to mineral leasing (California State
Lands Commission parcels in PAD-US) are added in as a separate
jurisdiction. State land is administered by the CSLC under
California Public Resources Code §6890, not the federal 1872
Mining Law, so the popup for a state parcel routes the user to
the CSLC application process instead of a federal claim form.

A simpler version of the same pipeline (intersect federal surface,
subtract active claims, stop) misses several categories of closed
ground. Each of the subtractions above came from working through
BLM's actual mineral-entry algorithm in detail.

**Scoring stability.** Each component of a parcel's score is a
percentile rank against a fixed reference distribution, not
against the current scan's cohort. This means adding a new
exclusion layer or fixing a bug in an old one removes the
encumbered parcels without shifting the scores of the survivors.
A parcel's score moves only when its underlying signal moves: a
new MRDS deposit nearby, an updated geology layer, a re-trained
ML model.

**Interactive map.** Layer toggles for jurisdiction (federal vs
state), withdrawals, geology, MRDS deposits, and topography.
Parcel navigator that cycles through ranked gap parcels or active
claims, opens the popup for each, and centers the map. Per-parcel
popup with the score breakdown plus one-click cross-check links
into BLM's GLO Records patent search and the MLRS Research Map.
Draw a polygon to score arbitrary ground against the same model.
Compare basket for side-by-side parcel analysis with CSV export.
MRDS filter by development status and grade.

## What this doesn't prove

A few categories the tool still doesn't catch:

- **Historic mineral patents.** Many Mother Lode sections had
  multiple patents issued out of federal title between 1870 and
  1925. BLM's GLO Records database has all of those documents,
  but the search interface is JavaScript-rendered and doesn't
  expose a public API, so the tool can't yet flag a parcel that
  overlaps a patented mining claim. The popup includes a
  pre-filled link into the GLO patent search for manual
  verification.
- **Split estate.** Where the surface was patented out long ago
  but federal mineral rights remain ("ALL MIN" in BLM's Master
  Title Plat notation), or vice versa ("NO MIN"), the Surface
  Management Agency layer doesn't expose the distinction.
  Catching this requires parsing the scanned Master Title Plats,
  which is a separate project (tracked as future work).
- **Placer-vs-lode signal.** The model is trained on a label that
  includes both lode and placer gold operations but is dominated
  by lode. The feature set has no drainage, basin, or
  alluvial-cover variables that would distinguish a placer target
  from a lode one. Using this for placer is reasonable as a
  starting point, not as the primary signal. A separate document
  in the repo lays out what a placer-specific model would need.

## Where to look

- **Live tool:** [johnsondevco.com/goldbug](https://www.johnsondevco.com/goldbug/)
- **Stack:** Python 3.13, FastAPI on Cloud Run for the
  polygon-scoring API, Folium + Leaflet for the map, GeoPandas +
  Rasterio for the data work, scikit-learn for the model.
